nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving, clear, warm lowland streams, ponds, and swampy areas with abundant aquatic vegetation and submerged woody debris; predominantly in the Milne Bay Province and southeastern Papua New Guinea

decorPreferences: caves and hollow ornaments for spawning (PVC pipe sections, ceramic caves, coconut shells)
dense planting (Java fern, Anubias, cryptocorynes, floating plants for diffused lighting)
driftwood and leaf litter for natural cover and tannin release

diet.notes: Primarily a live and frozen food enthusiast — daphnia, baby brine shrimp, mosquito larvae, micro-worms, and small bloodworms are ideal. Will generally accept high-quality frozen foods; can be trained onto micro-pellets and small sinking pellets with patience. Avoid large dry foods; small particle size essential. Wild fish prey on small invertebrates and zooplankton.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, harlequin rasboras)
dwarf freshwater shrimp (with caution — fry may be eaten)
avoidWith: boisterous fast-swimming fish that out-compete at feeding
other bottom-dwelling territorial fish competing for caves

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Tateurndina ocellicauda; shows typical adult male coloration with blue-grey body and red-spotted fins.
Wikimedia Commons image showing adult male peacock gudgeon with characteristic ocellated caudal spot.

summary: A jewel of Melanesian streams, the Peacock Gudgeon is a brilliantly-coloured nano sleeper-goby endemic to Papua New Guinea. Males display a blue-grey body adorned with red-spotted fins and a bold ocellated 'peacock eye' on the caudal peduncle. Cave-spawning, with dedicated paternal egg-guarding, makes it one of the most rewarding nano fish to breed.
careNotes:
The Peacock Gudgeon (Tateurndina ocellicauda) is a small Eleotridae sleeper-goby endemic to Papua New Guinea — notably NOT a true goby (family Gobiidae), though the common name 'Peacock Goby' persists in the hobby. It is one of the most colourful freshwater nano fish available, with males displaying an iridescent blue-grey body, rows of red and orange spots on the unpaired fins, a yellow belly, and a striking dark ocellus ('peacock eye') at the base of the caudal fin. Females are smaller, lack the pronounced cranial hump, and show less intense finnage colouration.

A species tank or planted community aquarium of at least 60 L is recommended. Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 5–15), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.8–7.8), warm (22–28°C), and well-filtered but with low flow — the species originates from slow, clear rainforest streams. Tannin-stained, dimly-lit setups with driftwood, leaf litter, and heavy planting closely replicate their native biotope and encourage bold behaviour and natural breeding.

Spawning caves are essential: provide PVC tubes (approximately 2–3 cm diameter), coconut cave ornaments, or dedicated cave décor to trigger breeding. Males actively compete for and defend cave sites; provide at least one cave per male plus extras to diffuse competition. Feed live or high-quality frozen foods (daphnia, baby brine shrimp, bloodworms, micro-worms) as a dietary staple — dry foods alone rarely keep fish in peak condition. Avoid overly large or boisterous tankmates; the peacock gudgeon is a mid-water hover-feeder that loses out to more aggressive feeders. Despite their small size and peaceful temperament, male conspecific rivalry over caves can be intense; a well-decorated tank with visual breaks reduces injury.
breedingNotes: Peacock Gudgeons are cave-spawners and one of the most readily-bred nano fish in the hobby. A conditioned pair will spawn readily in a cave without special intervention beyond suitable spawning sites and live or frozen food. The male fertilises eggs deposited inside the cave and assumes sole responsibility for guarding and fanning the clutch — remove the female if the male shows aggression post-spawning, or use a species-only breeding tank. A clutch typically contains 50–150 pale, adhesive eggs, which hatch in approximately 7–10 days at 25°C. The male guards the fry for several additional days after hatching. First foods: infusoria, green water, or commercial liquid fry food for the first week; then baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro-worms. Fry are tiny and require very gentle water movement during the early weeks. Multiple spawns per year are common with well-fed adults. Broodstock condition — particularly the feeding of live foods — noticeably improves spawn frequency and fry survival rates.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Tateurndina ocellicauda
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 7.5 cm), temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.8–7.8, hardness 36–215 ppm, native range (Papua New Guinea, Milne Bay and surrounding lowland drainages), biotope (slow clear vegetated lowland streams), spawning behaviour (cave-spawning, paternal egg guarding), and feeding notes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Peacock Gudgeon Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist-oriented care guide confirming tank size (20+ gallons / ~75 L recommended; 10 gal minimum for a pair), diet (live and frozen foods preferred, can train to dry), spawning cave requirements, and temperament (peaceful community fish). Notes the 'Peacock Goby' trade name misnomer — Eleotridae, not Gobiidae.

additional.name: FishBase — Tateurndina ocellicauda
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Eleotridae, Gobiiformes), native range (Papua New Guinea), maximum recorded size (7.5 cm TL), temperature range (22–28°C), and diet classification (carnivore). Confirmed lifespan approximately 4–6 years in captivity.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Peacock Goby species profile
additional.notes: Species profile confirming spawning behaviour details (male guards eggs 7–10 days to hatching), dimorphism notes (male cranial hump, female yellow belly stripe), and community compatibility. Confirms minimum tank 60 cm / 60 L for a pair.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Tateurndina ocellicauda feature
additional.notes: Historical hobbyist article on peacock gudgeon natural history and aquarium husbandry. Confirms PNG endemism, Eleotridae family placement, cave-breeding behaviour with paternal care, and notes on sexual dimorphism (male hump, red fin spotting more intense in males). Recommended as one of few vibrantly-coloured nano fish alternatives to dwarf cichlids.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawner; male guards and fans a clutch of 50–150 adhesive eggs for 7–10 days until hatching. Fry require infusoria/liquid fry food initially, then baby brine shrimp nauplii. Multiple spawns per year with live-food conditioning. Female should be removed or tank heavily decorated to prevent post-spawn male aggression.
